news_summary: Today's human rights progress highlights improved support for workers, refugees rebuilding lives, the unifying power of sports for social change, and urgent calls for global investment in Syria's recovery.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées enregistrées aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme mettent en lumière un soutien accru aux travailleurs, aux réfugiés qui tentent de reconstruire leur vie, le pouvoir fédérateur du sport en faveur du changement social, ainsi que les appels pressants en faveur d’investissements mondiaux pour la reconstruction de la Syrie.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve el mayor apoyo a los trabajadores, a los refugiados que reconstruyen sus vidas, el poder unificador del deporte para el cambio social y los llamamientos urgentes a la inversión mundial en la recuperación de Siria.

articles.title: New York City unveils its first rest stop for delivery workers
articles.content:
The day’s forecast called for high winds, but around midday in downtown Manhattan, it felt like a perfect spring day. The sun shone high in the sky last Tuesday as people gathered on the sidewalk around the corner from City Hall. Municipal employees mingled about, chatting excitedly. The cause for celebration wasn’t the weather — but a sleek, modernist-looking shed on the sidewalk where there had once stood a vacant newsstand.

The structure may not have looked like much, but it had been years in the making. Since 2021, Los Deliveristas Unidos — a union of app-based delivery workers — has been campaigning for the city to build outdoor structures where these workers can safely rest on the job, charge their e-bike batteries, and escape the elements.

The crowd had gathered for the opening of the first “deliverista hub,” which had been a long-discussed idea, until recently — when Mayor Zohran Mamdani decided to expedite the building process following years of permitting delays and red tape.

By the time several delivery workers dressed in jeans, tactical-looking jackets, and bike helmets showed up, the atmosphere was buzzing with excitement.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“This is what the public realm is made for,” New York City Parks Commissioner Tricia Shimamura said from behind a podium, standing next to the hub with its clear glass windows and tall metallic columns. “This is what it means for our city to serve the people who keep it running.”

In New York City, more than 80,000 men and women board their vehicles of choice every day and shepherd everything from burrito bowls to groceries right up to people’s front doors. Many of these deliveristas travel on two wheels, opting for e-bikes, scooters, and mopeds over cars. That means this workforce is more vulnerable to extreme weather events like heat waves and flash flooding — which are becoming more frequent and more severe due to climate change.

The idea for deliverista hubs sits at the intersection of workers’ rights and climate justice. If the city opens more of these facilities throughout the five boroughs, they will offer respite from all kinds of inclement weather, as well as provide a space for workers to talk to one another. Soon, the Lower Manhattan hub will also be staffed five days a week by a member of the Worker’s Justice Project, the worker center that helps organize Los Deliveristas Unidos. Delivery workers who are interested in learning more about joining the union can stop by the hub as a starting point.

At the grand opening, representatives from the New York City Parks Department, the delivery workers union, Worker’s Justice Project, and the Department of Transportation were present and gave speeches. New York Senator Chuck Schumer — who secured $1 million in federal funding for this hub — as well as New York City Public Advocate Jumaane Williams and City Council Member Shaun Abreu also spoke.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Throughout the proceedings, a fine mist fell over the attendees. But it hardly dampened the mood of the ceremony, with the crowd frequently breaking out into cheers.

Ligia Guallpa, the executive director of the Worker’s Justice Project, proudly claimed this week’s opening as a victory for the working class. By pushing this project through, she said, deliveristas are “redefining what’s possible” in cities like New York, where “public spaces have historically been built for cars, for the wealthy, and for the privileged.”

Frida Garza / Grist

Gustavo Ajche, the co-founder of Los Deliveristas Unidos, said he got the idea of repurposing city infrastructure to create places of rest for delivery workers during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic.

“We were really isolated,” Ajche told Grist, speaking in Spanish, “with nowhere to go. During the pandemic, everything was closed and things got very complicated — I saw my colleagues [working outside] struggling to find places to shelter from cold, rain, and wind.” But he noticed that there were pieces of urban infrastructure throughout the city that had sat abandoned for years — like vacant newsstands — that could easily help them escape the elements.

Still, the road from that moment to last week’s opening ceremony was anything but straightforward. Ajche lamented that New York City’s previous mayoral administration, led by Eric Adams, took its time when it came to approving permits required to construct the city’s first deliverista hub.

Under Mayor Zohran Mamdani, said Ajche, things have gone differently; The New York Times reported that the mayor pushed to open the hub within his first 100 days in office.

After years of delays, the physical construction of the deliverista hub only took about a month, said Ajche. Asked directly about the lengthy timeline by a reporter after speeches ended, Parks Commissioner Shimamura said, “Unfortunately, I can’t really speak [to that], I’ve only been the commissioner for the last eight weeks.”

Ajche acknowledged that just having one hub isn’t sufficient to provide for the tens of thousands of delivery workers who stream through the city every day. Both he and Shimamura demurred when asked about the possibility of opening more hubs throughout the five boroughs. It’ll depend on financing, they said. But establishing partnerships with other city agencies is also part of the challenge; Shimamura noted that not every viable spot for a deliverista hub is owned by the Parks Department.

While it’s formally open, the hub itself sat largely empty — no charging stations, no HVAC system. Those will come later, according to a representative from the Worker’s Justice Project. The mist that was still falling on the attendees, it turned out, wasn’t rain — just a drizzle from window-washers working on a building across the street. Nevertheless, some of the deliveristas posed for photos beside the shed. Standing with Guallpa, who had been handed a bouquet of flowers, the group sang out chants of “Deliverista! Power!” as many flashed grins for the cameras and lifted fists to the sky.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the opening of New York City's first rest stop for delivery workers, providing them with safe spaces to rest, charge e-bikes, and escape harsh weather. This initiative supports a large workforce vulnerable to climate impacts and labor challenges, representing a meaningful improvement in workers' rights and urban public space use. The story includes sufficient context about the campaign, city involvement, and the broader significance for labor and climate justice.
articles.personality_title: New York City opens its first rest stop for delivery workers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Delivery workers in New York City often face tough conditions, riding e-bikes and scooters through all kinds of weather. They have lacked safe places to rest, charge their vehicles, and shelter from rain, heat, or cold.

**What happened** – On a sunny spring day in downtown Manhattan, New York City opened its first “deliverista hub.” This small building replaces an old newsstand and offers delivery workers a place to rest, charge e-bikes, and escape bad weather. The project was pushed forward by a union called Los Deliveristas Unidos and supported by Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who sped up the process after years of delays.

**Impact** – This hub is the first of its kind in the city and helps protect over 80,000 delivery workers who bring food and groceries to people’s doors every day. It offers a safe spot to recover during long shifts and connects workers to resources like union support. The hub also highlights how public spaces can be designed to serve workers, not just cars or wealthy groups.

**What’s next step** – Although this first hub is now open, it still needs equipment like charging stations and heating or cooling systems. City officials and worker groups hope to create more hubs across New York’s five boroughs, but this will depend on funding and finding suitable locations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New York City’s first rest stop for delivery workers marks a new effort to improve their working conditions and create public spaces that support essential workers in all weather.
articles.personality_title_fr: New York inaugure son premier espace de repos pour les livreurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les livreurs à New York travaillent souvent dans des conditions difficiles, utilisant des vélos électriques et des scooters sous toutes sortes de temps. Ils n’avaient pas d’endroits sûrs pour se reposer, recharger leurs engins, ou se protéger de la pluie, de la chaleur ou du froid.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors d’une belle journée de printemps à Manhattan, New York a ouvert son premier « deliverista hub ». Ce petit bâtiment remplace un ancien kiosque à journaux et offre aux livreurs un lieu pour se reposer, recharger leurs vélos électriques et s’abriter du mauvais temps. Ce projet a été porté par un syndicat appelé Los Deliveristas Unidos et soutenu par le maire Zohran Mamdani, qui a accéléré le processus après des années de retard.

**Impact** – Ce hub est le premier du genre en ville et aide plus de 80 000 livreurs qui apportent chaque jour repas et courses aux portes des habitants. Il offre un endroit sûr pour récupérer pendant les longues journées de travail et met en lien les travailleurs avec des ressources syndicales. Ce lieu montre aussi comment les espaces publics peuvent être pensés pour les travailleurs, pas seulement pour les voitures ou les plus riches.

**Prochaine étape** – Bien que ce premier hub soit ouvert, il manque encore des équipements comme des stations de recharge et des systèmes de chauffage ou de climatisation. Les responsables espèrent ouvrir d’autres hubs dans les cinq arrondissements de New York, mais cela dépendra des financements et des emplacements adaptés.

**En résumé** – Le premier espace de repos pour livreurs à New York marque un nouveau pas pour améliorer leurs conditions de travail et créer des espaces publics qui soutiennent les travailleurs essentiels par tous les temps.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva York inaugura su primera parada de descanso para repartidores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los repartidores en Nueva York a menudo enfrentan condiciones difíciles, usando bicicletas eléctricas y scooters bajo todo tipo de clima. No tenían lugares seguros para descansar, cargar sus vehículos o protegerse de la lluvia, el calor o el frío.

**Qué pasó** – En un día soleado de primavera en Manhattan, Nueva York abrió su primer “deliverista hub”. Esta pequeña construcción reemplaza un antiguo puesto de periódicos y ofrece a los repartidores un lugar para descansar, cargar sus bicicletas eléctricas y protegerse del mal tiempo. El proyecto fue impulsado por un sindicato llamado Los Deliveristas Unidos y apoyado por el alcalde Zohran Mamdani, quien aceleró el proceso tras años de retrasos.

**Impacto** – Este hub es el primero en la ciudad y ayuda a más de 80,000 repartidores que llevan comida y compras hasta la puerta de las personas cada día. Ofrece un lugar seguro para recuperarse durante largas jornadas y conecta a los trabajadores con recursos sindicales. También muestra cómo los espacios públicos pueden diseñarse para apoyar a los trabajadores, no solo a los autos o a los grupos acomodados.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque este primer hub ya está abierto, aún falta equipo como estaciones de carga y sistemas de calefacción o aire acondicionado. Los funcionarios esperan crear más hubs en los cinco distritos de Nueva York, pero esto dependerá de financiamiento y lugares adecuados.

**Resumen en una frase** – La primera parada de descanso para repartidores en Nueva York representa un nuevo esfuerzo para mejorar sus condiciones laborales y crear espacios públicos que apoyen a los trabajadores esenciales en todo tipo de clima.
articles.image_prompt: A warmly lit, detailed painting of a sleek, modernist glass-and-metal urban shelter resembling a repurposed newsstand, surrounded by symbolic e-bikes and delivery bags resting nearby; silhouettes of diverse delivery workers gathered in camaraderie around the structure under a soft spring sky with gentle mist, capturing a sense of community, resilience, and support in a bustling cityscape.

articles.title: Fleeing Sudan's war, refugees rebuild their lives in Uganda
articles.summary: On the outskirts of the Ugandan town of Biale, tents are scattered along dirt roads that give way to open fields. The Kriandongo camp sits between a shattered past and a life tentatively being rebuilt. Here, the story does not end with fleeing war. Another phase begins, one where days are measured not in hours, but in the weight of loss and the effort to carry on.
articles.content:
Since war broke out in Sudan in April 2023, nearly 600,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived at the camp, some after gruelling journeys through several countries. They came with few belongings and many memories, trying to piece together lives that have been turned upside down.

UN News/Abdelmonem Makki Nearly 600,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived at the Kryandungo camp, some after long and arduous journeys spanning multiple countries.

Located about 275 kilometres from Kampala, Kriandongo is also home to refugees from South Sudan, Burundi, Kenya, the Democratic Republic of Congo and Rwanda, as well as the surrounding host community.

What unites them is greater than their differences: the experience of loss and the need to start again.

UN News travelled to the camp to see daily life up close and hear the most pressing challenges residents face since they fled war.

From engineering halls to a refugee tent

UN News/Abdelmonem Makki Hussein Hashim Taiman, a civil engineer with a master's degree, once worked with the civil engineering department of the UNAMID mission in Darfur.

Hussein Hashim Taiman is living a life he never imagined. A civil engineer with a master's degree, he once worked for the African Union-United Nations Hybrid Operation in Darfur (UNAMID).

Today, he sits inside a tent, serving as the head of the Sudanese refugee community in Kriandongo, one of the largest refugee camps in Uganda.

He fled Omdurman with his children in May 2023, travelling through South Sudan before reaching Uganda on a journey he describes as filled with fear, harassment and abuse.

“Here, when you complain, you find that someone else's misfortune is greater than yours, so you try to forget your own to help ease theirs,” he told us.

"We draw strength from each other. Sometimes we sit together as professionals and talk about our past. I am now living in a tent, but this is what war does."

The situation has not improved as many had hoped. Aid has dwindled and tents designed to last six months have become permanent shelter more than three years on.

Healthcare is inadequate, education irregular, and even water and food have become a shared responsibility among residents.

Hussein warns that the future of an entire generation is at stake and urged the United Nations and the international community to give greater attention to Sudanese refugees in Uganda.

Food: A matter of life and death

© Mutasim Mohamed Ahmed Mutasim Mohamed Ahmed, originally from the city of Nyala in South Darfur State, was living in Khartoum and working in trade between Sudan, China, and Dubai.

Faced with this harsh reality, simple initiatives emerged. Among them were community kitchens, which began as a response to an urgent need, before gradually becoming a lifeline that helped save Kriandongo refugees from starvation.

Out of this harsh reality, simple but far-reaching initiatives took hold, including community kitchens, which began as a response to an urgent need, has become a lifeline for Kriandongo's refugees.

Twenty such kitchens now operate within the camp, helping to reduce hunger and build social bonds among Sudanese residents.

Mutasim Mohamed Ahmed, from Nyala in South Darfur, once traded between Sudan, China and Dubai. The war upended that life, and he arrived in Uganda in 2023. Today he serves as secretary of the camp's community kitchens.

“These kitchens were set up after the World Food Programme cut food rations," he said. “We saw malnutrition among residents. There were deaths and miscarriages because of hunger.”

The work has changed him. “Working in community kitchens taught me to be human,” he said. “You see people hungry here, and if you don't have humanity inside you, you can't feel this. I feel it for my Sudanese people, they are my own flesh and blood.”

'We survived to help others'

© MDPD Dr. Wadad Makki (2nd left) travels long distances to reach the Kryandungo camp, because she has chosen to stand with those who live inside it.

From Kampala, where she now lives, Dr. Widad Makki regularly makes the long journey to Kriandongo camp, not because she has to, but because she has chosen to.

A former university professor and director of the Special Education Department in Khartoum State, she fled her home under bombardment.

“It was difficult to move my children through the gunfire, the smoke, the burning cars and the bodies in the streets,” she told UN News. “I used to ask them to cover their faces so they would not see.”

Now based in Kampala, Widad remains closely connected to the camp, visiting regularly through her work with the Al-Malam Darfur for Peace and Development organisation. She and her colleagues support community kitchens and provide meals for refugees at Kriandongo.

“We survived and arrived safely in Uganda, and now we are helping our Sudanese brothers and sisters in the camps,” she said.

But her concern about the prolonged crisis runs deep. “Our biggest fear is that this war will last a long time. There are so many challenges here: education, rent, living costs, no income, no jobs. We dream every day that the war will stop so we can return to Sudan.”

Refugee doctors on call

UN News/Abdelmonem Makki Dr. Abdul Jabbar Ahmed Adam, an internal medicine specialist, now works at the Gombe Hospital in Kampala, Uganda.

Away from the camps, the picture looks different in Kampala, where some Sudanese professionals have managed to rebuild their careers.

In one of the city's hospitals, Dr. Abdul Jabbar Ahmed Adam in his white coat treats patients of various nationalities.

The internal medicine specialist, who previously worked at Ibn Sina Gastroenterology Hospital in Khartoum, arrived in Uganda in 2023 and joined Gombe Hospital.

“Uganda received us warmly. The work here is good and there is no discrimination,” he said. “We haven't felt like strangers, we feel just as we did in Sudan.”

He was not alone. Several Sudanese medical professionals found the chance to keep practising in their host country. Some have opened their own clinics. “Uganda has good hospitals, but there is a shortage of staff,” Abdul Jabbar said. “That is where Sudanese doctors have contributed. Patients come to us from many different nationalities.”

He is also keen to challenge a common assumption. “Not everyone who comes here relies on aid,” he said. “There are professionals, traders and people with skills in many fields.”

White Heart hotel

UN News/Abdelmonem Makki Ibrahim Zakaria Yahya recounts the chapters of a long journey spanning years of displacement and attempts at resettlement.

Ibrahim Zakaria Yahya settled in Biale, near Kriandongo, long before the latest wave of displacement. He left Sudan's South Darfur in late 2007, reached Uganda in 2008 and, after five years in Kampala, moved to Biale where he says he was the first Sudanese to arrive with his family.

The early years were hard. “I suffered a lot when I first arrived,” he recalled. But gradually he found his footing – trading, farming, then moving into property until he had built a business of his own.

Three years ago, he opened a hotel he named The White Heart. “I chose that name as an invitation to purify hearts and consciences, to overcome the bitterness Sudanese people have lived through during repeated wars,” he explained.

“Anyone who comes here should come with a pure heart. We fled from war, and we need to coexist here so we can one day return home safely.”

Ibrahim is grateful to Uganda. “They welcomed me with open arms. I never felt like a refugee. I am only a refugee on paper, I have the same rights as citizens, and I even own land I was unable to own in Sudan.”

Yet he longs for home and hopes peace will come soon, so that he and the thousands still in Kriandongo can return.

For those inside the camp, that hope is what remains. The future is uncertain, services are scarce and anxiety runs deep, especially among children and young people. But people hold on.

Until the war ends, life here continues with its simplicity, its hardship and its endless stories.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant real-world impact of refugees fleeing war in Sudan and rebuilding their lives in Uganda, showing resilience, community initiatives, and professional contributions that benefit a large displaced population. It focuses on a single, meaningful topic with detailed context about the challenges and hopeful actions within the refugee camp and host community, illustrating a broader humanitarian and social significance.
articles.personality_title: Sudanese refugees rebuild lives and community in Uganda's Kriandongo camp
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Since a war began in Sudan in April 2023, nearly 600,000 people have fled to Uganda seeking safety. Many traveled through several countries before arriving at Kriandongo camp, about 275 kilometers from Uganda's capital, Kampala. The camp also hosts refugees from other nearby countries.

**What happened**
At Kriandongo, refugees face many challenges like limited food, healthcare, and education. Despite this, they have started community kitchens to fight hunger and support each other. Professionals among the refugees, like engineers and doctors, have taken leadership roles and even continue their work in Uganda. For example, a civil engineer named Hussein, who fled with his children, now leads part of the Sudanese community in the camp. Another refugee, Dr. Abdul Jabbar, works as a doctor in Kampala, helping many patients.

**Impact**
This story shows how refugees are not just surviving but actively rebuilding their lives and communities. The community kitchens reduce hunger and create social bonds, while professionals contribute skills that help both refugees and Ugandans. These efforts improve daily life in difficult conditions and offer hope to a generation affected by war.

**What's next step**
Refugees and aid groups hope for more support from the international community to improve living conditions in the camp. Meanwhile, refugees like Ibrahim, who opened a hotel called The White Heart, continue to build businesses and dream of peace in Sudan so they can return home.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Sudanese refugees in Uganda’s Kriandongo camp are rebuilding their lives through community support, professional skills, and hope for peace despite ongoing challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les réfugiés soudanais reconstruisent leur vie et leur communauté au camp de Kriandongo en Ouganda
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Depuis le début de la guerre au Soudan en avril 2023, près de 600 000 personnes ont fui vers l’Ouganda pour trouver refuge. Beaucoup ont traversé plusieurs pays avant d’arriver au camp de Kriandongo, situé à environ 275 kilomètres de Kampala, la capitale ougandaise. Le camp accueille aussi des réfugiés d’autres pays voisins.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Au camp de Kriandongo, les réfugiés font face à de nombreux défis comme le manque de nourriture, de soins médicaux et d’éducation. Malgré cela, ils ont lancé des cuisines communautaires pour lutter contre la faim et s’entraider. Des professionnels parmi eux, comme des ingénieurs et des médecins, occupent des rôles de leadership et continuent même leur travail en Ouganda. Par exemple, Hussein, un ingénieur civil qui a fui avec ses enfants, dirige une partie de la communauté soudanaise au camp. Un autre réfugié, le Dr Abdul Jabbar, travaille comme médecin à Kampala, aidant de nombreux patients.

**Impact**
Cette histoire montre que les réfugiés ne se contentent pas de survivre, ils reconstruisent activement leur vie et leur communauté. Les cuisines communautaires réduisent la faim et créent des liens sociaux, tandis que les professionnels apportent des compétences qui profitent à la fois aux réfugiés et aux Ougandais. Ces efforts améliorent la vie quotidienne dans des conditions difficiles et offrent de l’espoir à une génération affectée par la guerre.

**Prochaine étape**
Les réfugiés et les groupes d’aide espèrent un soutien accru de la communauté internationale pour améliorer les conditions de vie dans le camp. En attendant, des réfugiés comme Ibrahim, qui a ouvert un hôtel nommé Le Cœur Blanc, continuent de créer des entreprises et rêvent de la paix au Soudan pour pouvoir rentrer chez eux.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Les réfugiés soudanais du camp de Kriandongo en Ouganda reconstruisent leur vie grâce au soutien communautaire, aux compétences professionnelles et à l’espoir de paix malgré les difficultés persistantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Refugiados sudaneses reconstruyen sus vidas y comunidad en el campamento Kriandongo en Uganda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Desde que comenzó la guerra en Sudán en abril de 2023, casi 600,000 personas han huido a Uganda en busca de seguridad. Muchos viajaron por varios países antes de llegar al campamento Kriandongo, a unos 275 kilómetros de Kampala, la capital de Uganda. El campamento también acoge refugiados de otros países vecinos.

**Qué pasó**
En Kriandongo, los refugiados enfrentan muchos desafíos como la falta de comida, atención médica y educación. A pesar de esto, han creado cocinas comunitarias para combatir el hambre y apoyarse mutuamente. Profesionales entre los refugiados, como ingenieros y médicos, han asumido roles de liderazgo y continúan trabajando en Uganda. Por ejemplo, Hussein, un ingeniero civil que huyó con sus hijos, ahora lidera parte de la comunidad sudanesa en el campamento. Otro refugiado, el doctor Abdul Jabbar, trabaja como médico en Kampala, ayudando a muchos pacientes.

**Impacto**
Esta historia muestra cómo los refugiados no solo sobreviven, sino que reconstruyen activamente sus vidas y comunidades. Las cocinas comunitarias reducen el hambre y crean lazos sociales, mientras que los profesionales aportan habilidades que ayudan tanto a refugiados como a ugandeses. Estos esfuerzos mejoran la vida diaria en condiciones difíciles y ofrecen esperanza a una generación afectada por la guerra.

**Próximo paso**
Los refugiados y grupos de ayuda esperan recibir más apoyo de la comunidad internacional para mejorar las condiciones de vida en el campamento. Mientras tanto, refugiados como Ibrahim, que abrió un hotel llamado El Corazón Blanco, continúan construyendo negocios y sueñan con la paz en Sudán para poder regresar a casa.

**Resumen en una frase**
Los refugiados sudaneses en el campamento Kriandongo de Uganda están reconstruyendo sus vidas mediante apoyo comunitario, habilidades profesionales y esperanza de paz a pesar de los desafíos continuos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a humble refugee camp with warm earth tones, featuring interconnected tents symbolizing community and resilience, a simple communal cooking area with steaming pots representing shared meals and hope, and a winding path leading from distant war-torn lands toward a brighter horizon, all rendered with soft, natural colors and gentle silhouettes conveying strength, unity, and the rebuilding of lives.

articles.title: Why the ‘beautiful game’ is joining the fight for a better world
articles.summary: The world of football met the world of diplomacy this week as Brazilian World Cup legend Zico touched down at UN Headquarters in New York.
articles.content:
The sporting icon wasn't there to talk tactics, but to lead his lifelong club, Flamengo, into a global pact to tackle some of the planet’s biggest challenges.

Through an initiative called Football for the Goals, the UN is tapping into the sport’s massive cultural influence to promote human rights, environmental sustainability, and social equality.

A legendary partnership

Zico, a veteran of three World Cups and a hero to millions of fans worldwide, was named the first Brazilian Champion for the programme.

His role is simple but ambitious: use his status to inspire fans to think about the world beyond the pitch.

Zico told our UN News Portuguese team in an interview on Monday, that to be at the UN for the initiative was “a moment of great honor and much satisfaction that represents all my dedication, my career, and the way I conduct the profession I chose.”

He added that it was important to him to be representing the sport and the players on the pitch: “It is not a personal tribute, but rather a tribute to football itself, and what it represents."

While the UN often deals with complex policy and strategy, this partnership is about making global goals feel local.

By bringing a club with the massive reach of Flamengo on board, the UN hopes to turn match-day passion into real-world action.

Amazing Generation Generation Amazing has partnered with the United Nations since 2020 and is part of the UN initiative known as “Football for the Sustainable Development Goals.”

More than just a game

For those who don't scour the league tables, the scale of this collaboration is significant. Flamengo isn't just a sports team; it is a cultural institution with tens of millions of supporters.

“Flamengo’s decision to join Football for the Goals underscores the power of football in South America, especially in Brazil, to drive social progress and inspire collective action,” noted Melissa Fleming, the UN’s head of global communications.

"By bringing one of the world’s most influential clubs into this global initiative, we are strengthening a movement that connects the passion of the game with the urgent need to advance the Sustainable Development Goals and build a more just, inclusive and sustainable world for all.”

A goal for 2030

© FFTG

The goals in question aren't the ones found at the back of a net. They refer to the Sustainable Development Goals – the ambitious 17-point plan agreed upon by world leaders to end poverty and protect the environment by 2030.

By joining the movement, Flamengo has pledged to:

Go green: Implement more sustainable practices in how the club is run.

Implement more sustainable practices in how the club is run. Speak up: Use its massive social media presence to advocate for equality and human rights.

Use its massive social media presence to advocate for equality and human rights. Lead by example: Show fans and athletes alike how small changes in daily life can contribute to a healthier planet.

Why football?

The UN recognizes that football – or soccer as the US would have it – is the most global and accessible sport in the world. From dusty community pitches to gleaming international stadiums, it speaks a language everyone understands.

With icons like Zico leading the charge, the hope is that the same energy fans use to cheer on their team can be harnessed to protect the world they live in.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant collaboration between a globally influential football club and the UN to promote the Sustainable Development Goals, including human rights, environmental sustainability, and social equality. This partnership leverages the broad cultural impact of football to inspire large-scale social progress and advocacy, representing a meaningful and tangible positive impact on society.
articles.personality_title: Football club Flamengo teams up with UN to promote global goals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Football is the world’s most popular sport, loved by millions everywhere. The United Nations (UN) wants to use football’s power to help solve big problems like poverty and climate change.

**What happened** – Brazilian football star Zico visited the UN in New York to support a new partnership. His club, Flamengo, joined a UN program called Football for the Goals. This program encourages football clubs to promote human rights, protect the environment, and support equality.

**Impact** – Flamengo is not just any club; it has tens of millions of fans. By joining this effort, Flamengo will use its influence to spread important messages about caring for the planet and treating people fairly. Zico’s involvement helps inspire fans to think beyond the game and take action in their communities.

**What's next step** – Flamengo plans to adopt greener practices in running the club and use its social media to raise awareness. The UN hopes other clubs will follow Flamengo’s example, turning football’s excitement into real progress toward the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals by 2030.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Flamengo and the UN are working together to turn football’s worldwide passion into a force for positive change in society and the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le club de football Flamengo s'associe à l'ONU pour promouvoir les objectifs mondiaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le football est le sport le plus populaire au monde, apprécié par des millions de personnes. Les Nations Unies (ONU) souhaitent utiliser la puissance du football pour aider à résoudre de grands problèmes comme la pauvreté et le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La star brésilienne Zico a visité l'ONU à New York pour soutenir un nouveau partenariat. Son club, Flamengo, a rejoint un programme de l'ONU appelé Football for the Goals. Ce programme encourage les clubs de football à promouvoir les droits humains, protéger l'environnement et soutenir l'égalité.

**Impact** – Flamengo n'est pas un club ordinaire ; il compte des dizaines de millions de fans. En rejoignant cet effort, Flamengo utilisera son influence pour diffuser des messages importants sur la protection de la planète et le respect des personnes. L'implication de Zico aide à inspirer les fans à penser au-delà du jeu et à agir dans leurs communautés.

**Prochaine étape** – Flamengo prévoit d'adopter des pratiques plus écologiques dans la gestion du club et d'utiliser ses réseaux sociaux pour sensibiliser. L'ONU espère que d'autres clubs suivront l'exemple de Flamengo, transformant l'enthousiasme du football en progrès concret vers les Objectifs de développement durable d'ici 2030.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Flamengo et l'ONU travaillent ensemble pour transformer la passion mondiale du football en une force de changement positif pour la société et l'environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: El club de fútbol Flamengo se une a la ONU para promover objetivos globales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El fútbol es el deporte más popular del mundo, amado por millones de personas. Las Naciones Unidas (ONU) quieren usar el poder del fútbol para ayudar a resolver grandes problemas como la pobreza y el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – La estrella brasileña Zico visitó la ONU en Nueva York para apoyar una nueva alianza. Su club, Flamengo, se unió a un programa de la ONU llamado Football for the Goals. Este programa anima a los clubes de fútbol a promover los derechos humanos, proteger el medio ambiente y apoyar la igualdad.

**Impacto** – Flamengo no es un club cualquiera; tiene decenas de millones de seguidores. Al unirse a este esfuerzo, Flamengo usará su influencia para difundir mensajes importantes sobre cuidar el planeta y tratar a las personas con justicia. La participación de Zico ayuda a inspirar a los fans a pensar más allá del juego y a actuar en sus comunidades.

**Próximo paso** – Flamengo planea adoptar prácticas más ecológicas en la gestión del club y usar sus redes sociales para crear conciencia. La ONU espera que otros clubes sigan el ejemplo de Flamengo, convirtiendo la emoción del fútbol en progreso real hacia los Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible para 2030.

**Resumen en una frase** – Flamengo y la ONU trabajan juntos para transformar la pasión mundial por el fútbol en una fuerza para el cambio positivo en la sociedad y el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, warm-toned painting of a large, stylized football stadium shaped like a globe, with flowing green vines and leaves wrapping around it symbolizing environmental sustainability, while abstract silhouettes of cheering fans in Flamengo’s red and black colors radiate outward, their raised arms transforming into glowing rays that connect to icons representing human rights and social equality, all set against a soft, natural-colored background.

articles.title: ‘Now is the moment to invest’: Syria needs global backing, says UN expert
articles.summary: Syria has made “remarkable progress” on transitional justice within the past year, raising hopes for accountability and recovery after more than a decade of civil conflict.
articles.content:
While the determination of Syrians to rebuild their country following the overthrow of the Assad regime in December 2024 has been widely recognised, experts caution that sustained international engagement is essential to keep the transition on track amid ongoing humanitarian challenges.

A UN Team of Experts on the Rule of Law and Sexual Violence in Conflict has been working in Syria to support national institutions in investigating and prosecuting conflict-related sexual violence, while also strengthening broader rule-of-law systems.

Speaking to UN News last week, Sofia Candeias, Judicial Affairs Officer with the team, highlighted both the advances made and the significant challenges that remain, particularly in addressing sexual violence in wartime, which was widespread and systematically used during Syria’s brutal civil conflict.

“It is remarkable that in one year, the Syrians were able to put all of this in place,” she said.

Yet beneath this progress lies a far more fragile reality – one defined by weakened institutions, widespread trauma, and a race against time to translate commitments into meaningful accountability.

The UN continues to support a Syrian-led, Syrian-owned political process in line with Security Council resolutions 2254 and 2799.

Building from the ground up

The UN Team of Experts, established by Security Council resolution 1888 in 2009, has been working in Syria since the fall of the Assad regime in December 2024. The team is co-led by the Department of Peace Operations (DPO), the UN Human Rights Office (OHCHR), the UN Development Programme (UNDP) and the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General on Sexual Violence in Conflict, and its mandate is to support national institutions to strengthen the rule-of-law response to sexual violence and conflict.

Deployed three times, the team has worked closely with key Syrian bodies, including the National Commission for Transitional Justice, the Commission for Missing Persons, multiple ministries, and civil society organisations.

UNTOE Two UN Experts on the Rule of Law and Sexual Violence in Conflict (Sofia Candeias on the far left) meet the Minister of Justice of Syria (second from right) to discuss the rule of law response.

This approach, building national ownership while providing technical support, is central to ensuring that justice efforts are sustainable.

“Civil society organisations are really the backbone of what transitional justice will be,” Ms. Candeias said. “They are also the only way to truly listen to survivors.”

Syrian civil society groups have spent years documenting human rights violations, which may form the evidentiary foundation for future prosecutions.

Systematic and unfathomable

One of the greatest challenges facing Syria’s justice process is also one of its least visible: the true scale of conflict-related sexual violence.

“The honest assessment is that we don’t know the full scale of the problem, and we probably never will,” Ms. Candeias said.

What is known is deeply alarming. Sexual violence was widespread and systematically used throughout the conflict, in detention centres, at checkpoints, and during displacement. It was not incidental, but deliberate, she added.

“There are many more cases than those that have actually been documented,” she noted, pointing to stigma as a major barrier preventing survivors from coming forward.

“This was a systematic enterprise and there was a deliberate tactic to target and to use sexual violence to humiliate and to punish civilians”, Ms. Candeias underlined.

Men and boys targeted too

While women and girls were disproportionately affected, men and boys were also heavily targeted, particularly in detention. One Syrian partner organisation found that 98 per cent of detained men and boys in its dataset reported experiencing sexual violence – although even this figure likely underrepresents the reality, as those are just documented cases.

© UNOCHA/Ali Haj Suleiman Women and children walk through the Al Hol Camp in Syria, a displacement site housing over 30,000 people (file).



Staying silent

Stigma is widely seen as the single greatest obstacle to justice.

It operates at multiple levels: internally, as survivors struggle with shame; within communities, where disclosure can lead to rejection; and socially, where silence often prevails. As a result, many survivors never speak out.

To address this, the UN and its partners emphasise the need to create conditions in which survivors feel safe to seek help, which begins with public recognition, and a proactive attitude, “searching for the victims, and ensuring they have the conditions to come forward.”

“Acknowledging the gravity of sexual violence, and that is a crime, puts the scene on the side of the perpetrators, which is the first step.” Ms. Candeias said.

Equally important is the creation of “safe spaces”, where survivors can access medical care, psychosocial support, and legal assistance without fear of stigma or retaliation. What constitutes a safe space, however, varies across communities, making local engagement critical.

Progress without capacity

Despite institutional advances, Syria’s ability to deliver justice remains severely constrained. Years of conflict have hollowed out the country’s medical, forensic and judicial systems.

Without adequate forensic capacity, evidence cannot be properly collected or preserved. Without trained investigators and prosecutors, cases cannot move forward. Without psychosocial support, survivors are less likely to come forward at all.

“What we want is the citizens to be able to vindicate their rights, because a survivor is not only a survivor, but also an agent, a citizen,” Ms. Candeias pointed out.

A narrowing window

Compounding these challenges is a decline in international funding.

Support for civil society organisations - many of which lead documentation efforts and provide frontline assistance to survivors - has decreased over the past year. Funding for national institutions also remains limited.

For Ms. Candeias, this trend is deeply concerning.

“With so much progress in one year, now we need the technical response to be there,” she said. “The groundwork has been done by the Syrians, but we need more services.”

© UNFPA Syria Outreach workers in central-western Syria.

The risk is not only stagnation, but reversal.

If resources fail to materialise, evidence may be lost, survivors may disengage, and early trust in emerging institutions could erode, potentially setting back accountability efforts for years.

Pivotal moment

Syria now stands at a critical juncture.

Its rapid progress on transitional justice presents a rare opportunity to pursue accountability after years of conflict. But that opportunity remains fragile, dependent on sustained support.

“This is the moment to invest,” Ms. Candeias said. “We cannot fail to deliver what they have managed to put forward.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant progress in transitional justice and accountability efforts in Syria after years of conflict, highlighting tangible advances in addressing sexual violence and strengthening rule-of-law institutions. This progress has broad significance for Syrian society and victims of wartime abuses, and the story provides detailed context on the achievements and ongoing challenges, making it an inspiring example of recovery and justice.
articles.personality_title: Syria makes progress on justice after conflict but needs global support
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Syria has faced more than a decade of civil war that caused widespread suffering, including many cases of sexual violence. After the Assad regime fell in December 2024, Syrians began working toward justice and rebuilding their country.

**What happened** – In the past year, Syria has made important steps in transitional justice, especially in investigating sexual violence during the war. A UN team has helped Syrian institutions and civil groups collect evidence and support survivors. This team works with Syrian authorities to strengthen laws and ensure those responsible can be held accountable.

**Impact** – This progress is unique because it shows Syrians’ determination to face difficult truths and seek justice despite trauma and weak institutions. Sexual violence was used as a weapon during the war, affecting women, girls, men, and boys. Many survivors stay silent because of stigma, so creating safe spaces and public support is crucial. The work done so far lays a foundation for lasting justice.

**What’s next step** – Continued international funding and technical help are needed to keep this momentum. Without support, evidence might be lost, survivors might give up, and institutions could weaken. The UN and Syrian partners want to build on recent gains to ensure survivors get medical, legal, and psychological care and that justice processes continue.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Syria has taken important steps toward justice for war crimes, but ongoing global support is essential to protect survivors and strengthen the rule of law.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Syrie progresse en matière de justice après le conflit mais a besoin du soutien mondial
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Syrie a traversé plus d’une décennie de guerre civile ayant entraîné de nombreuses souffrances, notamment des cas importants de violences sexuelles. Après la chute du régime Assad en décembre 2024, les Syriens ont commencé à travailler pour la justice et la reconstruction de leur pays.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Au cours de la dernière année, la Syrie a réalisé des avancées importantes en matière de justice transitionnelle, notamment dans l’enquête sur les violences sexuelles durant la guerre. Une équipe de l’ONU a aidé les institutions syriennes et les groupes civils à collecter des preuves et à soutenir les survivants. Cette équipe collabore avec les autorités syriennes pour renforcer les lois et garantir que les responsables soient tenus pour compte.

**Impact** – Ces progrès sont uniques car ils montrent la détermination des Syriens à affronter des vérités difficiles et à chercher la justice malgré les traumatismes et des institutions fragiles. Les violences sexuelles ont été utilisées comme arme pendant la guerre, touchant femmes, filles, hommes et garçons. Beaucoup de survivants restent silencieux à cause de la stigmatisation, donc créer des espaces sûrs et un soutien public est essentiel. Le travail accompli pose une base pour une justice durable.

**Prochaine étape** – Un financement international continu et une aide technique sont nécessaires pour maintenir cet élan. Sans soutien, les preuves pourraient être perdues, les survivants pourraient abandonner, et les institutions pourraient s’affaiblir. L’ONU et ses partenaires syriens veulent s’appuyer sur ces acquis récents pour garantir aux survivants un accès aux soins médicaux, juridiques et psychologiques, et pour poursuivre les processus judiciaires.

**Message clé en une phrase** – La Syrie a fait des pas importants vers la justice pour les crimes de guerre, mais un soutien mondial durable est essentiel pour protéger les survivants et renforcer l’état de droit.
articles.personality_title_es: Siria avanza en justicia tras el conflicto pero necesita apoyo global
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Siria ha vivido más de una década de guerra civil que causó mucho sufrimiento, incluyendo muchos casos de violencia sexual. Tras la caída del régimen de Assad en diciembre de 2024, los sirios comenzaron a trabajar por la justicia y la reconstrucción de su país.

**Qué pasó** – En el último año, Siria ha logrado avances importantes en justicia transicional, especialmente en la investigación de la violencia sexual durante la guerra. Un equipo de la ONU ha ayudado a las instituciones sirias y grupos civiles a recolectar pruebas y apoyar a las víctimas. Este equipo trabaja con las autoridades para fortalecer las leyes y asegurar que los responsables rindan cuentas.

**Impacto** – Este progreso es especial porque muestra la determinación de los sirios para enfrentar verdades difíciles y buscar justicia a pesar del trauma y las instituciones debilitadas. La violencia sexual fue usada como arma en la guerra, afectando a mujeres, niñas, hombres y niños. Muchas víctimas guardan silencio por el estigma, por eso crear espacios seguros y apoyo público es vital. El trabajo hecho crea una base para una justicia duradera.

**Próximo paso** – Se necesita financiamiento internacional continuo y ayuda técnica para mantener este avance. Sin apoyo, se podrían perder pruebas, las víctimas podrían rendirse y las instituciones podrían debilitarse. La ONU y sus socios sirios quieren aprovechar estos logros para asegurar que las víctimas reciban atención médica, legal y psicológica y que continúen los procesos judiciales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Siria ha avanzado en justicia por crímenes de guerra, pero necesita apoyo global constante para proteger a las víctimas y fortalecer el estado de derecho.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sturdy, ancient olive tree with deep roots intertwined with fragments of broken scales of justice and delicate, glowing threads weaving through the branches, symbolizing healing, resilience, and the rebuilding of law and trust in a war-torn land, rendered in warm, earthy tones and soft natural colors.
